Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted by my extracurriculars or just feel too anxious to start. I'm a hands-on learner, so sitting and reading doesn't work great for me—I need to do something physical with the material. Most weeks I'd end up cramming in about 14 hours total, but it was scattered and disorganized. I'd often study late at night when my anxiety was keeping me up anyway.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and how I was feeling mentally. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework, then suddenly realize something was due and panic-study. When I had extracurricular commitments, those always seemed to take priority because at least there I felt like I was actually doing something. Regular schedules sound nice in theory, but my anxiety made it hard to stick to anything consistent.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or seeing notifications pile up that made me log in. Sometimes my peer support group would message about something happening on the LMS, and that would remind me to check. I also logged in when I was feeling guilty about not keeping up, even if I didn't actually do much once I was there. The anxiety of not knowing what I was missing was sometimes worse than just logging in to see.
Q4: For a while, yeah, checking the discussion boards became kind of routine because I actually enjoyed the peer interactions. Talking to other students felt less intimidating than the actual coursework. It happened naturally when I found a few people in the class I connected with. But the routine was more about the social aspect than the academic stuff—I'd log in to chat and then maybe glance at course materials while I was there.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really helpful because I could watch them multiple times and pause when I needed to. I watched over 250 minutes of them, which is way more than I expected. The discussion forums were also good because hearing how other students understood things helped me figure it out too. Anything interactive or social felt more useful than just reading documents alone.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the practice assignments. Readings just don't stick for me—I need to be moving or doing something with my hands to learn. I'd download PDFs with good intentions and then never open them again. Some resources felt too overwhelming, especially when my anxiety was high, so I'd just avoid them entirely rather than stress myself out more.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it involved interacting with other people. Like, discussion posts felt worthwhile because I got responses and felt connected. Anything that was just passive consumption felt like a waste unless there was a clear payoff, like it being directly on the exam. With limited energy and resources, I had to be picky about where I put my effort.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford to care about, honestly. Required stuff already stressed me out enough—adding optional work on top would've sent my anxiety through the roof. But weirdly, sometimes optional discussions were more engaging because there was no pressure. When something was required, it felt like a threat hanging over me; optional stuff I could approach on my own terms, if at all.
Q9: Oh yeah, tons of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study them on my phone during commutes or while doing other things. I also borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't always afford my own materials. A lot of my learning happened through hands-on practice and talking things through with peers in person, none of which would show up online.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd meet up with classmates from my peer support group and we'd work through problems together on paper. I'd also listen to downloaded lectures while exercising or doing chores—that kinesthetic movement actually helped me retain information better. My best studying often happened away from screens entirely.
Q11: Definitely. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then never go back to the online version. My internet isn't always reliable at home, so downloading was just practical. I'd rewatch lectures offline multiple times, but the LMS would only show that initial access. It probably looked like I barely engaged with materials I actually studied pretty thoroughly.
Q12: Mid-semester was really rough. My anxiety spiked when assignments started piling up, and I just froze. I'd log into the LMS, see everything I was behind on, and feel so overwhelmed that I'd close it without doing anything. My extracurriculars were also demanding more time, and with limited resources, something had to give. The course felt like it was moving too fast for me to catch up.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or extracurriculars, I'd log in but just scroll mindlessly without actually processing anything. Confusion was a big one too—when I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help because I was embarrassed. My anxiety made everything feel harder than it probably was. Some weeks I was just surviving, not really learning.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but be too anxious to actually focus on it, just watching the minutes tick by. Or I'd open discussion threads and read them without really absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel less guilty about not engaging, but I wasn't actually present mentally. My body was there but my brain was somewhere else worrying.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like, the longer I waited, the bigger the task seemed in my head. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd feel even worse. It's not that I didn't care; I cared too much, and that paralyzed me. My assignment completion rate shows how bad this got.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then scramble to get something done. The LMS would probably show these spikes of activity right before due dates. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing stronger than my avoidance. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When it was encouraging, it helped a little with my motivation. But critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and made me want to avoid that type of work in the future. I didn't always check feedback right away because I was scared of what it might say. The peer support helped more than official feedback, honestly—hearing from classmates felt safer.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for my learning style. I needed more flexibility and hands-on activities, but a lot of it was watching and reading. Having five online courses at once was overwhelming—I couldn't keep track of everything across all of them. The structure didn't account for students like me who learn better by doing than by consuming content passively.
Q19: The discussion forums and peer interaction opportunities made engagement easier—those were lifelines for me. But the heavy reading load and the pace of new content made it harder. I also struggled with how everything was organized; sometimes I couldn't find what I needed and gave up looking. More interactive, hands-on elements would have helped me engage better with the actual material.
Q20: That sounds familiar, honestly. You might be logging in constantly because of anxiety, checking if you missed something, reading discussions, watching videos—but then the actual assignments feel too overwhelming to complete. The LMS activity might show someone who's present but struggling to convert that presence into finished work. It's not laziness; it's often fear or confusion or both.
Q21: Yeah, I think some students just learn differently. If you study offline a lot, or you're efficient with your time, your LMS activity might look low even though you're actually doing well. Some people don't need to rewatch videos ten times or post in every discussion to understand the material. The LMS only captures one type of engagement, and it's not the only valid kind.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement completely. They can't tell if you're anxiously scrolling or deeply focused. They miss offline studying, peer conversations, the emotional experience of learning. They show behavior but not understanding or effort or struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. For me, engagement happens when I'm doing something active—discussing with peers, working through problems, applying concepts. It's not just logging in or watching videos; it's being mentally and emotionally present. True engagement feels like curiosity, not obligation, but that's hard to maintain when you're anxious and stretched thin.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything—the hours I spent worrying instead of working, the assignments I understood but couldn't bring myself to submit. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me going, or the offline studying that actually helped me pass. It definitely wouldn't show that I cared about learning even when my behavior suggested otherwise. My engagement was real; it just didn't always look like engagement from the outside.
